text.title: Jenkins Basics: Pipeline Types, Agent Types, and JCasC
text.summary: Pipeline type comparison (Declarative vs Scripted), agent types, essential plugins, and JCasC requirement.

rule.statement: Use Declarative Pipeline syntax for all new Jenkinsfiles; use Scripted only when Declarative cannot express the required logic (rare).
rule.rationale: Declarative pipelines support syntax validation at load time, restart-from-stage, and the `post` section for structured cleanup. Scripted pipelines offer more flexibility but provide no static validation — errors appear only at runtime, making debugging harder.

example.note: Minimal Declarative pipeline skeleton with required sections.

antipattern.description: Using Scripted Pipeline for a standard build-test-deploy workflow.
antipattern.reason: Declarative covers 95% of use cases with better tooling support. Scripted pipelines are harder to read, have no syntax validation, and cannot restart from a failed stage.

rule.statement: Never run builds on the Jenkins controller node; set controller executors to 0 and run all material work (clone, build, test) on agents.
rule.rationale: Builds on the controller consume resources needed for scheduling and plugin processing, causing job queue delays and potential OOM kills of Jenkins itself. Security-wise, builds should never have direct access to the controller filesystem where credentials are stored.

rule.statement: Use Kubernetes agents for dynamic, ephemeral build environments; they scale to zero when idle and provide isolation via pod-level resource limits.
rule.rationale: Kubernetes agents are created on demand and destroyed after the build, eliminating the stale-state problem of long-lived agents. Each build gets a clean environment with no pollution from previous builds.

rule.statement: Manage all Jenkins configuration (security realm, authorization, node definitions, shared libraries, credentials) via the JCasC plugin from a YAML file stored in source control.
rule.rationale: Manual Jenkins configuration is not reproducible. After a disaster recovery restore, JCasC re-applies the entire configuration from YAML, restoring Jenkins to its exact state in minutes. Without JCasC, rebuilding a Jenkins controller requires hours of manual UI configuration.

list.item: Configuration as Code (JCasC) — reproducible Jenkins configuration
Credentials Binding — secure credential injection without echoing to logs
Git + GitHub Branch Source — SCM integration and multibranch pipeline
Prometheus Metrics — metrics export for Grafana dashboards
Pipeline Utility Steps — JSON/YAML parsing, file operations in pipelines
